items.prompt: Who wrote 'The Road Not Taken' (1916), which contains the lines 'Two roads diverged in a yellow wood' and 'I took the one less traveled by'?
items.explanation:
Published 1916 in *Mountain Interval*; Frost's most famous poem, though widely misread
Most readers take it as a celebration of nonconformity; Frost intended it as gentle mockery of a friend (Edward Thomas) who always second-guessed his choices
Four-time Pulitzer Prize winner (1924, 1931, 1937, 1943) — more than any other American poet
Read a poem at John F. Kennedy's 1961 inauguration — the first poet to participate in a U.S. presidential inauguration
Died 1963 at age 88; born in San Francisco but associated entirely with rural New England
Also wrote 'Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening,' 'Mending Wall,' 'Fire and Ice,' and 'Birches'

items.prompt: Who wrote 'The Waste Land' (1922), the landmark Modernist poem that opens 'April is the cruellest month'?
items.explanation:
Published October 1922 in *The Criterion*; widely considered the most important English-language poem of the 20th century
Five-part, 434-line poem dense with allusions to Dante, the Bible, Shakespeare, Sanskrit, and dozens more
Ezra Pound heavily edited the manuscript, cutting it roughly in half; Eliot dedicated it to Pound as 'il miglior fabbro' ('the better craftsman')
'April is the cruellest month' deliberately inverts Chaucer's opening to *The Canterbury Tales* ('Whan that Aprill with his shoures soote')
Won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1948; born in St. Louis but became a British citizen
*Old Possum's Book of Practical Cats* (1939) became Andrew Lloyd Webber's musical *Cats* (1981)

items.prompt: Who wrote 'Howl' (1956), the Beat Generation poem that begins 'I saw the best minds of my generation destroyed by madness, starving hysterical naked'?
items.explanation:
Published 1956 in *Howl and Other Poems* by City Lights Bookshop; defining text of the Beat Generation
Publisher Lawrence Ferlinghetti was arrested for obscenity in 1957; his acquittal was a landmark First Amendment victory
Dedicated to Carl Solomon, whom Ginsberg met in a psychiatric hospital — the poem chronicles the destruction of talented people by conformist society
First read publicly at the Six Gallery reading in San Francisco, 1955, alongside Philip Whalen, Gary Snyder, and Michael McClure
Founding figure of the Beat movement alongside Jack Kerouac and William S. Burroughs
Also wrote 'Kaddish' (1961), an elegy for his mother Naomi, and 'America' (1956)

items.prompt: Who wrote 'The Raven' (1845), the narrative poem with the refrain 'Quoth the Raven, Nevermore'?
items.explanation:
Published January 1845 in the *New York Evening Mirror*; made Poe immediately famous across the United States
A grieving man is visited by a raven that only says 'Nevermore'; the refrain drives him to madness thinking of his lost love Lenore
Poe described his compositional method in 'The Philosophy of Composition' (1846): every decision was calculated for psychological effect
Poe also invented the detective story with 'The Murders in the Rue Morgue' (1841) and pioneered American horror fiction
Died in Baltimore in 1849 at age 40 under mysterious circumstances — possibly rabies, election 'cooping,' or alcohol
Also wrote 'Annabel Lee,' 'The Bells,' 'Eldorado,' and the story 'The Fall of the House of Usher'

items.prompt: Who wrote 'Do Not Go Gentle into That Good Night' (published 1951), the villanelle with the refrain 'Rage, rage against the dying of the light'?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1947, published 1951 in *In Country Sleep and Other Poems*; Thomas's most celebrated poem
A villanelle — a strict 19-line form with two refrains — addressed to his dying father, David John Thomas
Urges his father to resist death with full intensity rather than accepting it peacefully
Used in films including Christopher Nolan's *Interstellar* (2014), where Michael Caine's character recites it
Welsh poet known for rich musical language; also wrote *Under Milk Wood* (1954), a radio play set in a Welsh coastal village
Died in New York, November 1953, aged 39, following a drinking session at the White Horse Tavern
Bob Dylan took his stage name from Dylan Thomas
items.cardFront: 'Do Not Go Gentle into That Good Night' (1951)

items.prompt: Who wrote 'Ozymandias' (1818), the sonnet about a shattered desert statue with the inscription 'Look on my Works, ye Mighty, and despair!'?
items.explanation:
Published January 1818 in *The Examiner*; written as a friendly competition with Horace Smith, who wrote a rival sonnet on the same theme
Ozymandias is the Greek name for the Egyptian Pharaoh Ramesses II; inspired by a fragment of a colossal statue arriving at the British Museum
The contrast between the boastful inscription and the surrounding ruins became iconic for the futility of worldly power
A Second Generation Romantic poet alongside Keats and Byron; expelled from Oxford for co-authoring *The Necessity of Atheism* (1811)
Drowned 1822 at age 29 in a sailing accident in the Gulf of Spezia, Italy; Byron and Leigh Hunt cremated his body on the beach
Also wrote 'Ode to the West Wind' (1819), 'To a Skylark' (1820), and *Prometheus Unbound* (1820)

items.prompt: Who wrote 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' (1819), which ends with the lines 'Beauty is truth, truth beauty, — that is all / Ye know on earth, and all ye need to know'?
items.explanation:
Published January 1820 in *Annals of the Fine Arts*; one of Keats's five great odes written in 1819 — his most productive year
The urn depicts frozen scenes of pursuit and ceremony; Keats meditates on how art preserves beauty that life cannot sustain
'Thou still unravish'd bride of quietness' — the opening line addressing the urn
Trained as a surgeon but abandoned medicine for poetry; dismissed by some critics as a 'Cockney poet'
Died of tuberculosis in Rome in February 1821 at age 25 — one of the shortest-lived major English poets
Friend Percy Bysshe Shelley elegized him in *Adonais* (1821); Keats is buried in the Protestant Cemetery in Rome
Also wrote 'Ode to a Nightingale,' 'La Belle Dame sans Merci,' and 'To Autumn,' all from 1819

items.prompt: Who wrote 'Because I could not stop for Death –' (c. 1863), which personifies Death as a courtly carriage driver escorting the speaker to the grave?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1863; first published 1890 in *Poems*, a posthumous collection edited by Mabel Loomis Todd
Death is personified as a gentleman coachman riding with the speaker and 'Immortality' past schools, fields, and finally the grave
Dickinson wrote nearly 1,800 poems; only ~10 were published in her lifetime, most anonymously
Known for unconventional use of dashes, slant rhyme, and compressed imagery; stylistically unlike any 19th-century contemporary
Almost never left her Amherst, Massachusetts home as an adult; wore only white; maintained an extensive correspondence
Also wrote 'I heard a Fly buzz – when I died –', 'Tell all the truth but tell it slant –', and 'Hope is the thing with feathers'
Ranked alongside Walt Whitman as one of the two foundational figures of American poetry
items.cardFront: 'Because I could not stop for Death –' (c. 1863)

items.prompt: Who wrote 'Song of Myself,' the long free-verse poem published in *Leaves of Grass* (1855), containing the line 'I am large, I contain multitudes'?
items.explanation:
Published 1855 in the first edition of *Leaves of Grass*; Whitman revised it through his lifetime, producing a final 'deathbed edition' in 1891–1892
'I celebrate myself, and sing myself' — opening line; the poem is a sprawling celebration of the self, democracy, and America
Ralph Waldo Emerson called the first edition 'the most extraordinary piece of wit and wisdom that America has yet contributed'
Served as a volunteer nurse during the Civil War, visiting Washington D.C. hospitals; documented in *Drum-Taps* (1865)
Also wrote 'O Captain! My Captain!' (1865, elegy for Lincoln) and 'When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd' (1865)
Considered the father of free verse in American poetry; his influence extends to virtually every subsequent American poet

items.prompt: Who wrote 'The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock' (1915), which begins 'Let us go then, you and I, / When the evening is spread out against the sky'?
items.explanation:
Published 1915 in *Poetry* magazine; Eliot's first major published poem, written in his early twenties
Follows the neurotic Prufrock as he deliberates about attending a social gathering; he never actually arrives
'Do I dare to eat a peach?' and 'I have measured out my life with coffee spoons' — defining lines of Modernist alienation
'I have heard the mermaids singing, each to each. / I do not think that they will sing to me' — concluding image of romantic defeat
Ezra Pound helped get it published after editor Harriet Monroe was reluctant about its unconventional form
The epigraph is from Dante's *Inferno* — Guido da Montefeltro speaks only because he believes the listener cannot escape hell to repeat it
Proposed the prototype of the modern anti-hero: paralyzed by self-consciousness and dread of social judgment
items.cardFront: 'The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock' (1915)

items.prompt: Who wrote 'If—' (1910), the poem of fatherly advice ending 'you'll be a Man, my son!'?
items.explanation:
Published 1910 in *Rewards and Fairies*; voted the UK's favorite poem in a 1995 BBC poll
Written as advice from a father to his son; models the qualities of the ideal English gentleman — stoicism, integrity, resilience
Each stanza begins 'If you can...' followed by a virtue tested under difficult circumstances
Inspired in part by Dr. Leander Starr Jameson, leader of the failed Jameson Raid (1895) against the Boer South African Republic
Kipling was the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature (1907); also wrote *The Jungle Book* (1894)
Also wrote 'Gunga Din,' 'Mandalay,' and *Kim* (1901); long associated with British imperialism, his reputation has been reassessed
Famous lines: 'If you can meet with Triumph and Disaster / And treat those two impostors just the same'

items.prompt: Who wrote 'The Second Coming' (1919), which contains the lines 'Things fall apart; the centre cannot hold' and 'What rough beast, its hour come round at last, / Slouches towards Bethlehem to be born'?
items.explanation:
Published 1919 in *The Dial* and collected in *Michael Robartes and the Dancer* (1921); written in the wake of WWI and the Russian Revolution
Draws on Yeats's occult system of 'gyres' — spiraling historical cycles; 2,000 years of Christian civilization ending, a new 'rough beast' arriving
'The best lack all conviction, while the worst / Are full of passionate intensity' — the most quoted lines from 20th-century poetry
The phrase 'Things fall apart' became the title of Chinua Achebe's 1958 novel
Won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1923; the only Irish poet to win it
A leading figure of the Irish Literary Revival; also a senator of the Irish Free State and co-founder of the Abbey Theatre
Also wrote 'The Lake Isle of Innisfree,' 'Easter, 1916,' and 'Sailing to Byzantium'

items.prompt: Who wrote Sonnet 18, which begins 'Shall I compare thee to a summer's day? / Thou art more lovely and more temperate'?
items.explanation:
One of 154 sonnets published in *Shakespeare's Sonnets* (1609)
Promises the subject immortality through verse: 'So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see, / So long lives this, and this gives life to thee'
Part of the 'Fair Youth' sequence (Sonnets 1–126), addressed to an unnamed young man
Shakespeare's sonnets were likely written in the 1590s, circulated in manuscript, and published without his authorization
Sonnet 18 is among the most frequently recited and parodied poems in English
Also notable: Sonnet 130 ('My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun') deliberately subverts the conventional praise sonnet
items.cardFront: Sonnet 18 ('Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?')

items.prompt: Who wrote 'Dulce et Decorum Est' (published posthumously 1920), the WWI poem describing a gas attack and condemning the 'old Lie' that it is sweet and fitting to die for one's country?
items.explanation:
Written 1917–1918; published posthumously in *Poems* (1920) after Owen's death
'Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori' (Latin: 'It is sweet and fitting to die for one's country') — from Horace; Owen calls this 'the old Lie'
Describes a chlorine or mustard gas attack during WWI, followed by a vivid image of a dying soldier
Owen was killed in action on November 4, 1918, one week before the Armistice; he was 25 years old
Wrote most of his war poetry while recovering at Craiglockhart War Hospital, Edinburgh, where he met Siegfried Sassoon
Sassoon edited Owen's posthumous poems; the two are the defining British poets of WWI
Also wrote 'Anthem for Doomed Youth': 'What passing-bells for these who die as cattle?'

items.prompt: Who wrote 'The Tyger' (1794), the poem asking 'Tyger Tyger, burning bright, / In the forests of the night; / What immortal hand or eye, / Could frame thy fearful symmetry'?
items.explanation:
Published 1794 in *Songs of Experience*; companion piece to 'The Lamb' in *Songs of Innocence* (1789)
The tiger symbolizes the dark, ferocious forces of the universe; the poem asks whether the same God who made the innocent lamb could make this fearsome predator
Blake was a visionary Romantic poet, painter, and printmaker who self-published his illuminated poetry books using an engraving process he developed
Almost entirely unrecognized in his lifetime; his reputation was largely posthumous
Also wrote 'Jerusalem' ('And did those feet in ancient time'), 'London,' and the long prophetic poems *Milton* and *Jerusalem*
His marriage was childless; he reportedly showed his wife Catherine visions he claimed to see; she learned to engrave to assist his printing
Ranked by many critics as the first and most original of the English Romantic poets

items.prompt: Who wrote 'Still I Rise' (1978), the defiant poem with the repeated affirmation 'I rise' asserting Black dignity against historical oppression?
items.explanation:
Published 1978 in *And Still I Rise*; became one of the defining poems of African American literature
The refrain 'Still I rise' / 'I rise' answers each stanza's description of oppression with triumph and resilience
Read aloud by Michelle Obama at the 2021 U.S. Presidential Inauguration of Joe Biden
Angelou was also a memoirist, most famous for *I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings* (1969)
Read 'On the Pulse of Morning' at Bill Clinton's first inauguration (1993) — the first woman and first African American to write and deliver an inaugural poem
Named the Reynolds Professor of American Studies at Wake Forest University; held that position until her death in 2014 at age 86
Also wrote 'Phenomenal Woman' and 'Caged Bird'

items.prompt: Who wrote 'The Charge of the Light Brigade' (1854), commemorating the disastrous cavalry charge at the Battle of Balaclava with the line 'Into the valley of Death / Rode the six hundred'?
items.explanation:
Published December 9, 1854 in *The Examiner*; written within days of reading a newspaper report of the charge
The Light Brigade charged the wrong Russian artillery battery at Balaclava (October 25, 1854) due to confused orders; ~600 men rode into withering fire
'Theirs not to reason why, / Theirs but to do and die' — the most quoted lines from the poem
Tennyson was Poet Laureate of the UK from 1850 until his death in 1892 — the longest tenure in the role
Also wrote 'In Memoriam A.H.H.' (1850, elegy for his friend Arthur Hallam), 'Ulysses,' and 'The Lady of Shalott'
His poem 'Crossing the Bar' (1889), written when he feared death was imminent, was played at his state funeral
'Tis better to have loved and lost / Than never to have loved at all' is from *In Memoriam*

items.prompt: Who wrote 'Kubla Khan' (published 1816), the unfinished dream-vision poem said to have been composed during an opium-induced reverie, featuring 'Xanadu' and 'a stately pleasure-dome'?
items.explanation:
Allegedly written in 1797 during an opium trance; published 1816 at the urging of Lord Byron
Coleridge claimed he was composing 200–300 lines mentally when interrupted by 'a person from Porlock'; only 54 lines survived
The 'person from Porlock' has become a byword for unwanted interruption of creative flow
Opens with 'In Xanadu did Kubla Khan / A stately pleasure-dome decree' — among the most recognized opening lines in poetry
Kubla Khan was the real Mongol Emperor (grandson of Genghis Khan) who ruled China (1260–1294)
Coleridge also wrote 'The Rime of the Ancient Mariner' (1798) — source of 'Water, water, every where, / Nor any drop to drink'
A founding figure of English Romanticism alongside William Wordsworth; their co-authored *Lyrical Ballads* (1798) launched the movement

items.prompt: Who wrote 'This Be the Verse' (1971), the darkly comic poem that famously begins 'They fuck you up, your mum and dad'?
items.explanation:
Published 1971 in *New Humanist*; collected in *High Windows* (1974); Larkin's most-quoted poem
Describes a cycle of parental damage passed down through generations, ending with the advice not to have children
'Man hands on misery to man. / It deepens like a coastal shelf.' — the closing argument
Larkin was a librarian at the University of Hull for over 30 years; declined the Poet Laureateship in 1984
Also wrote 'Aubade,' 'The Whitsun Weddings,' and 'Annus Mirabilis' ('Sexual intercourse began / In nineteen sixty-three')
Characterized by precise observation of English provincial life, bleak wit, and unsparing honesty about mortality
Selected by the Times as the greatest British poet since WWII in a 2008 poll

items.prompt: Who wrote 'In Flanders Fields' (1915), the WWI poem in which fallen soldiers urge the living to 'Take up our quarrel with the foe' and pass on the torch?
items.explanation:
Published December 8, 1915 in *Punch* magazine; one of the most widely read poems to emerge from World War I
Opens: 'In Flanders fields the poppies blow / Between the crosses, row on row'
The poem's imagery of red poppies growing over soldiers' graves directly inspired the adoption of the poppy as a symbol of remembrance
McCrae was a Canadian physician and artillery officer; wrote the poem at the Second Battle of Ypres after the death of his friend Lt. Alexis Helmer
Died of pneumonia and meningitis in January 1918 at a Canadian military hospital in Boulogne-sur-Mer, France — never knowing the war's outcome
Canada's Remembrance Day (November 11) traditions are closely tied to McCrae's poem
The poem was used in Allied recruitment campaigns; McCrae himself was ambivalent about this use
